text: modern thought. It will moreover be indicated how this new order was in a critical sense liminal at its core, establishing fear and doubt (archetypical liminal experiences) as epistemic foundations. Chapter 6 engages a completely different empirical field, namely gaming and gambling practices. Starting from the premise that gambling practices have in recent decades come to permeate everyday life, it sets out to describe how gaming and gambling actually emerged and became institutionalized as an integral part of the general transition to modernity. The role played by Venice – a liminal space par excellence – will be discussed, as will be the figure of Casanova, who in a very direct way became a trickster agent in the spread of state-sponsored gambling. Chapter 7 will follow up on this perspective, by engaging one particular leisure activity which in the recent period came to enjoy a huge success: that of bungee jumping. This focus might seem random. However, a key feature of these jumping activities is the experience of danger: going to the limits, or indeed, standing on the limit. Indeed, in his classic work Man, Play and Games, Caillois identifies the crucial play-category called ilinx precisely with a willing movement of falling. Hence, bungee jumping (taking as a cue Caillois’ brief mention of the Mexican ‘voladores’; Caillois 2001: 23) serves as an extremely apt metaphor for understanding liminality in contemporary culture and leisure. It is here argued that Turner’s notion of the liminoid does not fully capture what is going on in contemporary leisure, characterized as it is by the almost panicking search for excitement and stimulation of the senses. Most of those ‘extreme’ activities that continue to proliferate involve no transformation of subjectivity or no passage to any ‘other world’. The bungee jumping ‘industry’ can thus be taken to signify a shift toward what we propose to conceptualize as the ‘limivoid’: the inciting of near-death experiences, a jump into nothingness, a desperate search for experience in a world of ontological excess. Into Liminality 17 Chapter 8 provides further substantiation of the liminal character of political modernity. The chapter advances a series of reasons why an anthropology of performance, following Turner, can enrich and supplement existing political science traditions in the study of political revolutions. Revolutions – including the ones that are marking our times – exemplify almost archetypical cases of a dissolution of order at the large-scale level. Modern political revolutions very much resemble rituals and must therefore be analysed within a processual approach. To study revolutions implies not only a focus on political behavior ‘from below’, but also the recognition of moments where high and low are relativized or subverted, and where the micro and macro levels fuse together in critical conjunctions. Revolutions are characterized by a deep and boundless contingency, where everything is ‘in play’. Contingency co-exists with highly utopian and eschatological forces, often spurred by the rise of new leaders who, we argue, cannot simply be understood by employing Weber’s notion of charisma. Chapter 9 concludes and draws together the perspectives presented throughout the eight preceding chapters. Summarizing and highlighting the critical ways in which liminality is imploding in the contemporary landscape (and here also with reference to the economic sphere), it will end on a discussion of one of the most basic terms relevant to a meaningful human life, but duly underdeveloped in social thought, namely that of ‘home’ and of ‘feeling at home’. If moving into liminality can best be captured as a loss of home and a ritualized rupture with the world as we know it, any movement out of liminality must somehow relate to a sort of home-coming, a feeling at home in the world and with the world, at the levels of both thought and practice. This page has been left blank intentionally PART I Retrieving Liminality Within the History of Social Thought: From Arnold van Gennep to Victor Turner and Beyond This page has been left blank intentionally Chapter 1 Arnold van Gennep: Fragments of a Life-Work at the Thresholds Qu’importe la mode d’activité, ce qui compte c’est l’homme. Arnold van Gennep, Le génie de l’organisation 1 This chapter is a carefully framed introduction to the concept of liminality, a concept that was proposed by Arnold van Gennep more than a century ago to analyse the middle stage in ritual passages; it is an introduction that serves to ground an understanding of what the concept meant to van Gennep, of how he actually addressed it in his classical work on ritual passages, and of the role of this book in the context of his life and work as ethnographer, folklorist and social scientist. However, this introduction must make more than formal mention of the man, Arnold van Gennep. It must do so for the simple reason that Arnold van Gennep is largely unknown as a writer and thinker. His 1909 book, Les Rites de Passage, became a classic when it was translated into English and published in 1960 as The Rites of Passage. The book has been widely quoted since and continues to be listed in almost any university course on religion. And yet, as a thinker, Arnold van Gennep is still today not really discussed outside a narrow circle of mostly French folklorists. Course syllabi on the sociology or anthropology of religion most often insert an excerpt from Rites of Passage on their reading list. In the standard approach, this chapter is discussed as a supplement to Durkheim’s theory of ritual and religion. The common reception is that van Gennep simply offered a useful terminology for the study of ritual passages, whereas it was Durkheim – and later others – who provided anthropologists and sociologists with a broader theoretical framework. In Routledge’s authoritative Key Thinkers, in the short entry on van Gennep (Karady 1987: 255), it characteristically says that van Gennep’s approach was ‘essentially empirical with limited theoretical underpinning’. The same judgment is everywhere to be found. Arnold van Gennep is not considered an important anthropologist beyond his classificatory achievement,
